data.title: Grand juries take on growing importance amid Trump's legal retribution campaign
data.description:
Grand juries are running interference with President Trump’s aggressive use of the Justice Department — including when it comes to going after his political foes.

The common phrase that a grand jury would indict a ham sandwich underscores the reputation of the panels as a rubber stamp of sorts for prosecutors.

But they haven’t always worked that way for Trump.

On Tuesday, a federal grand jury in Washington, D.C., refused to indict six Democratic lawmakers who participated in a November video calling on service members to reject “illegal orders.”  

Before that, grand juries in Norfolk and Alexandria, Va., declined to reindict New York Attorney General Letitia James (D) after a judge threw out the initial prosecution over the unlawful appointment of the U.S. attorney who pursued it.  

Grand juries in D.C., Chicago and Los Angeles also have rejected harsh charges for protesters of Trump’s use of federal probes.

Rizwan Qureshi, a former federal prosecutor with the U.S. attorney’s office for the District of Columbia, said the grand juries’ decisions directly reflect their assessment of probable cause — a strong signal the underlying evidence or legal theory did not warrant criminal charges.  

“A grand jury’s refusal to indict is rare, and it’s meaningful — not a political talking point,” he said. 

Grand juries convene behind closed doors, where prosecutors explain to them the law and present evidence. That could include witness testimony, as grand juries have subpoena power. 

After reviewing the evidence, grand jurors vote on whether to indict.   

Jessica Levinson, a law professor at Loyola Marymount University, noted that grand jurors don’t decide guilt or innocence, but they do provide a procedural hurdle before the case may proceed. 

“The case has to pass a little bit more than a laugh test,” she said.  

It’s typically an easy win for prosecutors, said Neama Rahmani, a former federal prosecutor and co-founder of West Coast Employment Lawyers, as all it takes is a simple majority agreeing to indict after the government presents a one-sided case.  

“If you can’t convince that captive audience, you are either a bad lawyer or you have a bad case,” Rahmani said.  

It’s rare for grand juries to return “no bills,” the shorthand for declining to return a bill of indictment. So rare, in fact, that the “ham sandwich” remark was coined by a former New York state chief judge back in 1985.

A 2016 Justice Department report, the most recent publication of such data, shows that grand juries refused to indict in just six cases of the 155,000 prosecutions the agency concluded.  

Both Qureshi and Rahmani said they “never once” received no bills. In fact, it never took more than a few minutes for grand jurors to sign the indictment, Rahmani added.  

“If you can’t even get a grand jury indictment, which is really a very small speed bump on the road to a criminal prosecution, there is absolutely no way you can then be able to convince 12 out of 12 jurors beyond a reasonable doubt — the highest standard under the law — with a defense attorney presenting a very different argument at trial,” Rahmani said. 

In some instances, prosecutors have stepped down rather than present to grand juries without enough evidence.  

Erik Siebert, the former interim U.S. attorney for the Eastern District of Virginia, stepped down rather than push forward with the prosecutions of James and former FBI Director James Comey, which he reportedly determined were too weak to indict.  

His successor, Lindsey Halligan, pressed on and secured indictments against both Trump foes, but they were later thrown out. The Justice Department has appealed.  

“I don’t think the grand jury system changed; the standards didn’t change,” Levinson said. “I think it would be fair to ask whether or not the types of cases that we’re asking to bring have been changed.”

Grand juries have still voted to indict in several politically charged or otherwise high-profile cases since Trump’s return to the White House.  

Prosecutors secured indictments against ex-national security adviser John Bolton over allegations of mishandling classified material and former Milwaukee Judge Hannah Dugan for helping an immigrant dodge federal authorities.  

They also succeeded at bringing formal charges against the man accused of planting pipe bombs outside the Democratic and Republican national committee offices on the eve of the Jan. 6, 2021; the man accused of shooting two National Guard members in D.C., killing one; and the man accused of gunning down two Israeli Embassy staffers, to name a few.  

Jeanine Pirro, the U.S. attorney for the District of Columbia, said in August that grand jurors “don’t take it so seriously,” suggesting they’ve grown “used to crime” in the nation’s capital and are unwilling to buy into her office’s efforts to clamp down. 

In Washington, Pirro’s office has seen a string of grand jury refusals in cases where felony charges were sought against residents who lashed out against Trump’s federal surge in the city.  

The case of D.C.’s “sandwich guy,” who slung a sandwich at a federal agent, ended in an acquittal on a misdemeanor count after a grand jury refused to return an indictment on the felony assault count prosecutors initially sought. In another case, three grand juries would not indict a woman on a federal assault charge tied to a tussle with an FBI agent. 

“I don’t really care about these people who say, ‘We don’t need you,’ or, ‘You’re going too hard,’” Pirro said last summer. “My job is to protect the victim, and that’s what we’re going to do. We’re going to make the criminal accountable.”  

Qureshi said the prevalence of politically charged prosecutions under Trump’s Justice Department is “jarring” but the recent decisions by grand juries show the “institutional safeguards” are working. 

“People are taking it seriously,” he said.

data.title: ICE raids can revive Democrats' multiracial coalition
data.description:
In recent weeks, Americans have been shocked by the harsh, and sometimes lethal, ICE raids fanning across U.S. cities. Democrats, independents and Republicans alike are turning away from President Trump’s violent and chaotic immigration enforcement. While the administration tries to defuse this crisis, Trump’s slumping poll numbers show this is a major concern for Republicans during a critical election year.

Democrats must seize this opening to rebuild their flailing electoral coalition to win in 2026 and beyond. Indeed, this moment is a vivid reminder that Latinos are just like other racial “minorities” — threatening and expendable when White Republicans consider it politically convenient. Democrats have an opportunity to respond with a revitalized multiracial coalition where Latinos are a central partner by highlighting the similarities that connect all people of color across the U.S.

In 2016, the multiracial coalition that powered Democrats’ electoral success through the Obama years began to falter, with many Latinos voting for the nativist Republican candidate, Donald Trump. Many experts worried that Latinos had voted against their self-interest. In 2024, the number of Latino Trump voters surged beyond the usual 33 percent that regularly vote Republican, leaving little doubt that a non-trivial number of Latinos — America’s largest ethnic minority — will vote for a candidate precisely because he is Republican and hostile to people of color, including their undocumented co-ethnics.

It’s time for a change in our perspective of Latino politics — and one that is clear-eyed about the connections between partisanship and ethnic identity. The overwhelming majority of Latinos — roughly 66 percent of them — identify as Democrats and hold liberal political opinions. Democratic leaders should leverage this fact to reenergize their multiracial electoral coalition. They can spark this transformation by weaving more compelling narratives about Latinos as team players in this quest for greater equity for all people of color.

Indeed, evidence from my research lab at UCLA shows that reminding Latinos about how the racial discrimination they encounter is similar (though not identical) to the discrimination faced by other racial “minorities” leads many Latinos — especially Democratic ones — to become significantly more supportive of pro-Black, pro-Asian and pro-Latino policies. These reminders of shared discrimination also turn Latinos into more steadfast supporters of Democratic candidates in national elections. Our data-driven evidence comes from nine large-scale experiments with Black, Asian, Latino and Middle Eastern and North African adults. It also comes from large waves of surveys that tracked the same Black, Latino and Asian adults before the 2024 election, after this presidential contest, and seven months into Trump’s turbulent second term.

Our evidence shows that the path to a revitalized Democratic coalition runs through solidarity between all people of color. It also clearly indicates that Latinos are predisposed to be reliable Democrats and active allies in collective struggles against a still racially unequal society. But like other voters who encounter a deluge of competing information and messages, they must be regularly and persuasively reminded about this critical role. Spelling out the implications of ICE raids for Latinos and other people of color is one clear channel to accomplish this.

Today, race relations are more complicated and combustible than before because racial identities are intertwined with partisanship. White Republicans see themselves on the losing side of a rising demographic tide of people of color, who together now make up 40 percent of the U.S. This is precisely why President Trump’s advisers have tried to make inroads with some people of color. Not to convert all people of color to their camp, but to loosen Democrats’ hold over them.

Democrats must respond in kind by actively encouraging Latinos to practice more solidarity with Black, Asian and other people of color based on shared grievances. In our electoral system, large, unified numbers of votes secure victories. A Democratic multiracial coalition can ensure that political tides lift all boats, including Latinos’.

The connecting tissue for these alliances starts with the ICE raids against Latinos and linking them explicitly to other Republican efforts that exclude African Americans (voter disenfranchisement), Asian Americans (curtailment of visas for legal immigrants), and other people of color. These grievances already have well-worn grooves in people of color’s collective memories because of the 1960s civil rights struggles. But Democrats have been risk-averse to organize contemporary electoral debates around race. Republicans obviously do not have any qualms about this and are winning.

Ultimately, seizing this opportunity means Democrats can more faithfully represent the political interests of African Americans, Asian Americans, Latinos and other “minorities.” These electorates do not agree on everything. But they do not have to. In the current battle over our multiracial democracy, racial unity is the true path back to power for these communities.

Efrén Pérez is professor of political science and psychology at UCLA, where he directs the Race, Ethnicity, Politics and Society Lab. He is the author of “Diversity’s Child: People of Color and the Politics of Identity.”

data.title: MAP: Which states have the most measles cases in 2026?
data.description:
(NEXSTAR) – Measles outbreaks in close to half of U.S. states are responsible for over 900 confirmed cases in 2026, according to the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC).

Less than two full months into the year, there have been five new outbreaks and the number of confirmed cases stands at 910 as of Feb. 12. The total count of confirmed cases in 2025 was 2,276, according to the CDC.

The mounting outbreaks, with the United States at risk of losing its measles elimination status, prompted a U.S. health official to speak out last week, despite his boss — Robert F. Kennedy Jr., the nation’s health chief — making past comments doubting the efficacy of vaccines.

“Take the vaccine, please,” said Dr. Mehmet Oz, the physician overseeing the Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services.

From Oz, there was a clear message on measles.

“Not all illnesses are equally dangerous and not all people are equally susceptible to those illnesses,” he told CNN’s “State of the Union.” “But measles is one you should get your vaccine [for].”

The 24 states that reported cases were Arizona, California, Colorado, Florida, Georgia, Idaho, Kentucky, Maine, Minnesota, Nebraska, New York, North Carolina, North Dakota, Ohio, Oklahoma, Oregon, Pennsylvania, South Carolina, South Dakota, Utah, Vermont, Virginia, Washington, and Wisconsin.

The country’s measles elimination status hinges on whether or not a single measles chain has spread uninterrupted within the U.S. for at least 12 months.

In 2000, measles were officially eliminated in the U.S. Because it wasn’t actively spreading, new cases only occurred when someone contracted the virus abroad and returned home, according to the CDC.

What makes measles dangerous?

There is little room for error in trying to stop measles. The virus is one of the most contagious, infecting 9 out of every 10 unvaccinated people exposed.

A person with measles who coughs or sneezes in a room, and then leaves that room, can still infect another person who enters that room up to two hours later, the CDC says.

Community-level protection takes a 95% vaccination rate.

The massive outbreak in Texas in 2025, for example, where the estimated vaccination rate is 93.2%, began with one patient who was suffering from a telltale rash on Jan. 20, 2025, according to state health department data.

From there, the outbreak exploded. Officially, 762 people fell ill, most of them in rural Gaines County, and two children died. Many more got sick and were never diagnosed: 182 potential measles cases among children in Gaines County went unconfirmed in March 2025 alone, state health officials said, a possible undercount of 44% in that county.

Measles, which often arrives with a high fever, cough, runny nose, red eyes and a rash, can be especially dangerous in children under the age of 5, who may develop pneumonia and encephalitis.

The CDC says the best way to protect yourself against measles is to take two doses of the measles, mumps and rubella (MMR) vaccine.

data.title: Massie: Bondi was 'afraid to' look at Epstein survivors at hearing
data.description:
Rep. Thomas Massie (R-Ky.) on Sunday said that Attorney General Pam Bondi did not look at the Jeffrey Epstein survivors during her testimony before a House panel because she was “afraid to.”

Co-host Martha Raddatz asked Massie on ABC News’s “This Week” if he was surprised that Bondi would not look at the Epstein survivors behind her.

“I think that was kind of cold on her part. I think she was afraid to,” Massie replied. “And look, these survivors would love to have a meeting. It’s not about Bill Clinton and it’s not about Donald Trump. This Epstein Files Transparency Act was about getting these survivors justice. We’ve got some degree of transparency but it’s called the Department of Justice, not the department of transparency.”

“And so, what these survivors need, they need to see some of their own 302 forms, which haven’t been released, and they also need to see some of the men that they’ve implicated prosecuted,” he added.

Bondi went head-to-head with lawmakers in a fiery hearing on Wednesday as the country’s top lawyer appeared before the House Judiciary Committee for the first time since taking office.

She fielded questions on issues ranging from pending investigations into President Trump’s adversaries to immigration, but the most tense exchanges came over the Epstein files.

Bondi repeatedly pushed back on lawmakers during the hearing, asking why they didn’t demand answers about the Epstein investigation under prior administrations, including former Attorney General Merrick Garland.

“I’m glad you’re asking about Merrick Garland,” Massie said during the Wednesday hearing.

“This is bigger than Watergate. This goes over four administrations. You don’t have to go back to Biden. Let’s go back to Obama. Let’s go back to George [W.] Bush. This cover-up spans decades, and you are responsible for this portion of it,” he added.

On Sunday, Massie said that Garland “performed better” than Bondi did during her testimony.

“I don’t think she did very well. She came with a book full of insults, one for each congressperson. She obviously had one for me,” Massie said.

“And, you know, I’ve been there when Merrick Garland was there. Obviously, politically, I don’t agree with him, but he performed much better in terms of at least not looking bad. And, unfortunately, we didn’t get the answers we wanted about the Epstein Files,” he added.

Massie also said he did not have confidence in Bondi after the hearing.

“I don’t think Pam Bondi has confidence in Pam Bondi. She wasn’t confident enough to engage in anything but name-calling in a hearing,” he told Raddatz on Sunday.

“She hasn’t got any sort of accountability there at the DOJ. When I asked her specifically who redacted Leslie Wexner’s name from the one document that mattered, she couldn’t give me an answer, she wouldn’t give me an answer. But ultimately, it’s her who is responsible for the document production according to our law, the attorney general.”

data.title: Homan says he doesn't 'know' meaning behind Noem 'right leaders' comments
data.description:
White House border czar Tom Homan said Sunday he didn’t “know” what Department of Homeland Security (DHS) Secretary Kristi Noem’s recent comments about ensuring “we have the right people voting, electing the right leaders to lead this country” meant.

“So, what does she mean when she says ‘electing the right leaders?’ That’s not really immigration enforcement or DHS responsibility,” CNN’s Jake Tapper asked Homan on “State of the Union.”

“I don’t know. That’d be a question for the secretary. If I had to guess, probably that — you know — only those legally eligible to vote would vote. But I have not talked to the secretary about those statements. That’d be something she’d have to answer,” Homan replied.

On Friday, Noem suggested that her department plays an expansive role in election security. The secretary alleged that she has the authority to find “vulnerabilities” in the election system and put in place “mitigation measures” to ensure local and state elections are carried out “correctly.”

In a press conference in Arizona, Noem claimed that elections were under DHS’s mission of “maintaining critical infrastructure.”

“I would say that many people believe that it may be one of the most important things that we need to make sure we trust, is reliable, and that when it gets to Election Day, that we’ve been proactive to make sure that we have the right people voting, electing the right leaders to lead this country through the days that we have, knowing that people can trust it,” she said.

Noem also said on Friday that she was “still in charge” of DHS, even as Homan recently gained more control over Trump’s immigration agenda.

DHS has faced heat in recent weeks over the two fatal shootings of Renee Good and Alex Pretti in Minneapolis by federal immigration authorities.

data.title: There's no such thing as a 'good shoot' — only credible investigations
data.description:
There are no “good shoots.” Although the term remains common in media and public discourse, it has been fading within law enforcement for a reason. Law enforcement officers have to live for the rest of their lives with the legal, professional and personal consequences of a split-second decision to use deadly force. This is particularly true when a death results. 

But when such shootings ignite an already politically divided country, the consequences extend far beyond any one agent or agency. That is why the Department of Justice should issue a detailed public investigative report on the two recent shootings that occurred in Minneapolis as part of Operation Metro Surge.

On Jan. 7, an Immigration and Customs Enforcement officer fatally shot Renee Good during an encounter in Minneapolis. Politicians, pundits and online commentators quickly staked out positions on whether the incident constituted a “good shoot,” often interpreting limited public video evidence through a political lens. Before that debate had settled in Minneapolis, Border Patrol agents shot Alex Pretti on Jan. 24. 

Assessments about whether the shooting was justified were widespread. Public statements by senior federal and state officials, along with the resignations of Department of Justice and FBI leaders connected to the Good investigation, further fueled doubts about whether any review could be impartial. These doubts make it harder for law enforcement to do its job, leave families searching for credible answers and deepen an already divisive atmosphere. 

Most people outside law enforcement do not fully understand the legal and tactical standards governing the use of deadly force. At the same time, many Americans are skeptical that any review of these incidents will be independent and impartial, particularly when senior political leaders publicly characterize events before investigations are complete.

This combination of misunderstanding, mistrust and preemptive judgment understandably leads many to doubt that a truly independent outcome is even possible. 

But perception does not have to become reality. We can still have thorough shooting investigations, followed by transparent public reports into these incidents, to create an authoritative record grounded in facts rather than speculation. 

Some voices within the administration appear to recognize this necessity. On the Pretti investigation, Border Czar Tom Homan said, “We’ll let the investigation play out and let it go where it goes.” This reflects an understanding that credibility, once lost, can only be rebuilt through restraint and transparency, not rhetoric.

The FBI and Justice Department Civil Rights Division confronted this challenge before with the 2014 investigation of Officer Darren Wilson’s shooting of Michael Brown in Ferguson, Mo. The public report issued in the Brown case demonstrated the value of laying out evidence and legal reasoning in full view. That approach did not persuade everyone, but it did establish a factual baseline and identify lessons that could lead to meaningful reform. 

The Minneapolis incidents are complex but not unprecedented. As in Ferguson, any investigation will unfold amid political pressure from all sides, community skepticism and the possibility of parallel state and local inquiries. Yet even under these conditions, such investigations can still produce credible and constructive results. 

Rather than focusing on a single piece of evidence or a brief video clip, shooting investigations examine the totality of the circumstances.

Investigators reconstruct what happened, why agents were present, what information they had at the time and how decisions were made in rapidly evolving situations. They assess physical and forensic evidence, review firearms training and use-of-force policies, analyze communications and timelines and evaluate witness statements for credibility and consistency. Medical and autopsy findings are considered alongside ballistic and scene evidence.  

Conclusions about criminal liability and other internal policies are of obvious concern to the agencies involved, the agents who fired their weapons and the families of those who died. But they also matter to the public. In a democracy, the question is not only whether a shooting was lawful, but whether the process used to reach that determination is worthy of trust. 

The Justice Department investigation into Brown’s death and the Ferguson Police Department illustrated both sides of that equation. It conclusively showed that the widely repeated “hands up, don’t shoot” narrative was false. At the same time, it exposed serious institutional failures that created conditions in which such a narrative could plausibly take hold. 

Dispelling falsehoods while confronting systemic problems may be one of the most important contributions a federal investigation can make. Another is identifying lessons learned, from improved tactical training for agents to better operational and oversight procedures for agencies. 

The value of a full and transparent investigation is not found in declaring a shooting good, justified or unjustified to universal satisfaction. A significant portion of the public will reject whatever conclusion is reached regardless of the outcome. The purpose instead is to replace speculation with facts and slogans with understanding. It provides a disciplined way to examine what happened, why it happened, and what could have been done differently. 

That process serves not only the officers and families directly affected, but also the public’s confidence in the rule of law. In a divided country, strengthening that confidence may be the most important outcome any investigation can deliver. 

Christopher M. Donohue is a retired FBI agent.

data.title: Massie says Garland performed 'much better' than Bondi after fiery House hearing
data.description:
Rep. Thomas Massie (R-Ky.) on Sunday said that former Attorney General Merrick Garland “performed better” than current Attorney General Pam Bondi did during her testimony before a House panel examining the Justice Department, including its handling of the Jeffrey Epstein investigation.

“I don’t think she did very well. She came with a book full of insults, one for each congressperson. She obviously had one for me,” he said on ABC News’s “This Week.”

“And, you know, I’ve been there when Merrick Garland was there. Obviously, politically, I don’t agree with him, but he performed much better in terms of at least not looking bad. And, unfortunately, we didn’t get the answers we wanted about the Epstein Files,” he added.

Bondi went head-to-head with lawmakers in a fiery hearing on Wednesday as the country’s top lawyer appeared before the House Judiciary Committee for the first time since taking office.

She fielded questions on issues ranging from pending investigations into President Trump’s adversaries to immigration, but the most tense exchanges came over the Epstein files.

Bondi repeatedly pushed back on lawmakers during the hearing, asking why they didn’t demand answers about the Epstein investigation under prior administrations, mentioning Garland.

“I’m glad you’re asking about Merrick Garland,” Massie said during the Wednesday hearing.

“This is bigger than Watergate. This goes over four administrations. You don’t have to go back to Biden. Let’s go back to Obama. Let’s go back to George [W.] Bush. This cover-up spans decades, and you are responsible for this portion of it,” he added.

On Sunday, Massie said that Bondi not looking at the Epstein survivors behind her during the hearing was “cold on her part” and likely because she was “afraid to do so.”

He also said he did not have confidence in Bondi after the hearing.

“I don’t think Pam Bondi has confidence in Pam Bondi. She wasn’t confident enough to engage in anything but name-calling in a hearing,” Massie told co-host Martha Raddatz on Sunday.

“She hasn’t got any sort of accountability there at the DOJ. When I asked her specifically who redacted Leslie Wexner’s name from the one document that mattered, she couldn’t give me an answer, she wouldn’t give me an answer. But ultimately, it’s her who is responsible for the document production according to our law, the attorney general.”

data.title: Schumer on Dems' DHS demands: 'These are common sense'
data.description:
Senate Minority Leader Chuck Schumer (D-N.Y.) said Sunday that Democrats’ demands for reforming the Department of Homeland Security (DHS) “are common sense,” just one day after funding for the department ran out.

“We need masks off, we need cameras on and we need every police — every [Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE)] officer, to be identified,” Schumer told CNN’s Jake Tapper on “State of the Union.”

“Jake, these are common sense proposals. They’re supported by the American people. Why won’t Republicans go for them? They don’t give any good answers. It’s something that every police department does across the country, but ICE is rogue, out of control,” the New York Democrat added.

In the last few weeks, DHS has been mired in controversy over President Trump’s strict immigration agenda. Fury directed at the department, which houses ICE and Customs and Border Protection, flared following two fatal shootings in Minneapolis by federal immigration authorities.

White House border czar Tom Homan announced last week that controversial Trump administration immigration crackdown efforts in Minnesota would come to an end.

DHS funding lapsed early Saturday morning, causing a third at least partial government shutdown in Trump’s second term. Democrats have demanded reforms to immigration enforcement practices, including tightening warrant requirements, prohibiting masks for federal immigration officers and ending roving patrols, with pushback from Republicans.

Sen. John Fetterman (D-Pa.), who has been known to buck his party in recent years, was the only Democratic senator to vote to advance a yearlong funding package for DHS last week. 

“This shutdown literally has zero impact on ICE functionality. Country over party is refusing to hit the entire Department of Homeland Security. Democracy demands a way forward to reform ICE without damaging our critical national security agencies,” Fetterman said in a post on the social platform X Friday.

The Hill has reached out to DHS for comment.

data.title: America can avoid a public health crisis by saving this one alert system
data.description:
U.S. policymakers use a secret weapon to uncover widespread threats to public health. This tool enabled them to discover that Americans had high blood lead levels from leaded gasoline. It warned them when Americans were showing the first signs of the obesity epidemic. It helped them learn about the millions with high blood pressure who never knew they had it. The vitamins in baby formula that help infants grow strong and the nutrients added to basic foods like cereal and bread exist because this tool revealed what Americans were missing. 

The tool, called the National Health and Nutrition Examination Survey, or NHANES, is a quiet surveillance system that alerts the nation to dangers we may not have seen otherwise.  

Yet today NHANES itself is imperiled. The Centers for Disease Control and Prevention has eliminated the entire team responsible for planning and coordinating the survey, threatening the most consequential health database in U.S. history. If NHANES collapses, we can no longer track the chemicals accumulating in children’s bodies, the vitamins and nutrients Americans are lacking, or the silent growth of chronic diseases like diabetes. We will lose the information that guides food‑labeling policy, environmental protections, and preventative medicine practices used in every doctor’s office. 

A country of 340 million people would, quite literally, no longer know the state of its own health. 

When the 43-day government shutdown hit in October, the CDC — like other agencies — had to rapidly assign employees to “essential” or “non‑essential” categories. The small team that designs and coordinates NHANES was swept into the non‑essential group and let go. After the shutdown, while most affected staff returned to their roles, the NHANES planners did not. Their positions simply vanished.  

Dismantling NHANES will hobble efforts to advance the goals of Make America Health Again, the administration’s signature initiative emphasizing prevention. NHANES provides crucial information to meet those goals. Without it, policymakers will be forced to steer blind.  

We would not have recognized rising mercury levels in women of childbearing age — information that has guided advice on safe seafood consumption for expectant parents. 

We would not have known that Americans carried measurable levels of pesticides, flame retardants and PFAS — forever chemicals now under intense federal scrutiny. 

Nor would we have detected the dramatic decline in children’s exposure to secondhand smoke, a major public‑health success story, until years later, long after the opportunity to understand what drove that progress. 

For more than six decades, Republicans and Democrats alike have supported NHANES because it gives policymakers the hard, apolitical facts needed to protect the nation’s health. At a time when few institutions command bipartisan trust, NHANES has endured precisely because it delivers objective, early warnings about what is happening in Americans’ bodies long before those problems appear in hospitals or headlines. 

Congress must now act to restore the NHANES team and safeguard the survey’s future.  

When Health and Human Services sought to terminate thousands of mental health and substance abuse grants in mid-January, bipartisan pressure from congressional committees forced the department to reverse course within 24 hours. Lawmakers can take similarly constructive steps here, supporting the reinstatement of the NHANES planning team, signaling in appropriations the importance of maintaining full survey capacity, requesting a public plan to ensure uninterrupted data collection, and seeking to clarify how the dismantling unfolded. A united front by Congress can prevent the dismantling of NHANES, the country’s most indispensable health‑monitoring system. 

The story of NHANES is, at its core, a story about what Americans can achieve when we choose to understand ourselves. Every breakthrough it enabled began with the simple act of paying attention. Restoring NHANES is about preserving our ability to learn from one another and build a healthier future together. 

At a moment when so much in public life feels polarized, the path forward here is refreshingly clear: we can simply decide to keep looking out for one another. 

Sean Hennessy is senior fellow at the Leonard Davis Institute of Health Economics at the University of Pennsylvania, where he evaluates the real-world effectiveness and safety of prescription drugs.

data.title: Musk's lunar city plan supercharges America's return to the moon
data.description:
Recently, SpaceX CEO Elon Musk made a history changing announcement on his social media platform X. It read, in part: “For those unaware, SpaceX has already shifted focus to building a self-growing city on the Moon, as we can potentially achieve that in less than 10 years, whereas Mars would take 20-plus years.”

“It is only possible to travel to Mars when the planets align every 26 months (six-month trip time), whereas we can launch to the Moon every 10 days (2-day trip time),” Musk explained. “This means we can iterate much faster to complete a Moon city than a Mars city.”

He concluded by affirming that he has not given up on Mars: “SpaceX will also strive to build a Mars city and begin doing so in about five to seven years, but the overriding priority is securing the future of civilization and the Moon faster.”

The announcement was not entirely a surprise for those who have been paying attention. Besides a greater ease of access than Mars, the moon provides SpaceX with a means of making a lot of money as a source of raw materials for Musk’s proposed orbiting AI data centers. The data centers would be powered 24/7 with solar panels.

The data centers, instead of launching from Earth, would be manufactured on the moon and launched by an electromagnetic mass driver into Earth orbit. Launch costs would be drastically reduced because of the moon’s lower gravity and the costs measured, in part, in electricity.

But the initial expense would be immense. SpaceX would have to launch mining and manufacturing equipment, not to mention the technology that lunar settlers will need to live, from the Earth to the moon. Doubtless, a lot of Optimus robots will be included in the initial cargo to help with construction. Of course, Musk would use as much local materials to build his lunar city as possible.

Musk is the richest human being on the planet and will be richer still once SpaceX undergoes its IPO later this year. If anyone can build a lunar settlement, he can. Whether he can do it inside of 10 years is another question.

Besides providing a means to build orbital AI data centers, Musk’s pivot to the moon provides several other benefits. If SpaceX builds “Moon Base Alpha,” as Musk has called it after the setting of a 1970s science fiction show, with mining and manufacturing infrastructure and a mass driver, it will be able to provide services to other customers, such as those building orbital industrial facilities by providing a source of raw and refined materials. A lunar city could spark a space-based industrial revolution.

Musk’s pivot to the moon places SpaceX squarely in line with the goals of the Artemis program: moon first and then Mars. NASA may wind up renting space at the SpaceX lunar city for its explorers and scientists at less cost than if it built one itself.

Ironically, the decision to build a lunar city first before going to Mars may make the latter easier in the long run. Deep craters at the lunar poles contain water ice that can be refined into oxygen and hydrogen. The hydrogen can be combined with carbon dioxide to make methane, which the SpaceX Starship uses as fuel. The process is known as a Sabatier reaction.

According to Phys.org, the deep crater cold traps at the lunar poles also contain frozen carbon dioxide. Carbon dioxide exhaled by the astronauts may be another source. Additionally, the first Starships to go to Mars may top off their fuel in the vicinity of the moon before heading on to the Red Planet, decreasing the cost and complexity of interplanetary travel.

One final benefit of Musk’s pivot to the moon will be for companies working on lunar mining and manufacturing technologies. Blue Origin, SpaceX’s rival, is working on something called Blue Alchemist, which would separate lunar regolith into materials such as oxygen, silicon, iron and aluminum. The silicon would be used to create solar cells.

People have dreamed about a lunar settlement even before the days of the Apollo program. NASA has twice failed, because of politics, to get one started. Ironically, an immigrant from South Africa may well cause the dream to take form.

Mark R. Whittington, who writes frequently about space policy, has published a political study of space exploration entitled “Why is It So Hard to Go Back to the Moon?” as well as “The Moon, Mars and Beyond” and, most recently, “Why is America Going Back to the Moon?” He blogs at Curmudgeons Corner.
